Apple Watch Heart Rate Analysis Notes

This version adds short explanations between code cells so the flow is easier to follow later.

  • The goal is to isolate Apple Watch heart rate records, clean the timestamps and values, and build a reasonable resting heart rate proxy without filling missing days.
  • Because the watch was not worn consistently, the analysis intentionally avoids interpolation. Missing days stay missing.
  • The derived daily_rest dataset uses the lowest 20% of heart rate values per day as a practical estimate of resting heart rate.
In [1]:
import xml.etree.ElementTree as ET
import pandas as pd
In [2]:
import os
os.getcwd()
Out[2]:
'C:\\Users\\arivu'
In [3]:
tree = ET.parse('export.xml')
root = tree.getroot()
In [4]:
records = []

for record in root.findall('Record'):
    records.append({
        "type": record.get('type'),
        "startDate": record.get('startDate'),
        "endDate": record.get('endDate'),
        "value": record.get('value'),
        "unit": record.get('unit'),
        "sourceName": record.get('sourceName'),
        "sourceVersion": record.get('sourceVersion')
    })

df = pd.DataFrame(records)
df.head()
Out[4]:
type startDate endDate value unit sourceName sourceVersion
0 HKQuantityTypeIdentifierDietaryWater 2020-11-26 19:03:33 -0500 2020-11-26 19:03:33 -0500 473.176 mL FitMenCook 129

4 HKQuantityTypeIdentifierDietaryWater 2020-11-28 14:04:00 -0500 2020-11-28 14:04:00 -0500 473.176 mL Simple 7.2.2.2011251424
In [5]:
hr = df[df['type'] == 'HKQuantityTypeIdentifierHeartRate']
hr.to_csv('heart_rate.csv', index=False)

1. Check the data sources first

This confirms the heart rate export contains records from both Nithya’s Apple Watch and Withings.

This step matters because imported Withings records live in the same Apple Health export, and mixing them would blur the trend you actually want to study.

In [33]:
hr['sourceName'].value_counts()
Out[33]:
Nithya’s Apple Watch    402091
Withings                 14461
Name: sourceName, dtype: int64

2. Isolate Apple Watch rows

This section creates hr_aw, a dataframe containing only the most common source name, which here corresponds to the Apple Watch.

That keeps the analysis focused on one device instead of combining measurements from multiple sources.

In [57]:
apple_name = hr['sourceName'].value_counts().idxmax()
hr_aw = hr[hr['sourceName'] == apple_name]
In [58]:
hr_aw.head()
Out[58]:
type startDate endDate value unit sourceName sourceVersion
15382 HKQuantityTypeIdentifierHeartRate 2021-04-18 14:07:47 -0500 2021-04-18 14:07:47 -0500 88 count/min Nithya’s Apple Watch 7.0
15383 HKQuantityTypeIdentifierHeartRate 2021-04-18 14:12:56 -0500 2021-04-18 14:12:56 -0500 81 count/min Nithya’s Apple Watch 7.0
15384 HKQuantityTypeIdentifierHeartRate 2021-04-18 14:13:01 -0500 2021-04-18 14:13:01 -0500 81 count/min Nithya’s Apple Watch 7.0
15385 HKQuantityTypeIdentifierHeartRate 2021-04-18 14:13:06 -0500 2021-04-18 14:13:06 -0500 81 count/min Nithya’s Apple Watch 7.0
15386 HKQuantityTypeIdentifierHeartRate 2021-04-18 14:13:10 -0500 2021-04-18 14:13:10 -0500 82 count/min Nithya’s Apple Watch 7.0

In [63]:
hr_aw = hr_aw.copy()
In [64]:
hr_aw['startDate'] = pd.to_datetime(hr_aw['startDate'], errors='coerce')
hr_aw['endDate']   = pd.to_datetime(hr_aw['endDate'], errors='coerce')

4. Remove timezone labels

This strips timezone information from the datetime columns after conversion.

For this notebook, that makes grouping and plotting simpler because the focus is the long-term trend, not timezone comparisons.

In [65]:
hr_aw['startDate'] = hr_aw['startDate'].dt.tz_localize(None)
hr_aw['endDate']   = hr_aw['endDate'].dt.tz_localize(None)
In [66]:
hr_aw.isnull().sum()
Out[66]:

endDate          0

endDate          datetime64[ns]
value                    object
unit                     object
sourceName               object
sourceVersion            object
dtype: object
In [69]:
hr_aw['value'] = pd.to_numeric(hr_aw['value'], errors='coerce')

5. Clean the heart rate values

After converting value to numeric, rows with invalid heart rate values are removed.

This avoids having text or blanks contaminate the summary statistics and later calculations.

In [73]:
hr_aw = hr_aw.dropna(subset=['value'])
In [74]:
hr_aw.dtypes
Out[74]:
type                     object
startDate        datetime64[ns]
endDate          datetime64[ns]
value                   float64
unit                     object
sourceName               object
sourceVersion            object
dtype: object
In [75]:
hr_aw['value'].describe()
Out[75]:
count    402091.000000
mean         90.672432
std          16.776711
min          41.000000
25%          79.000000
50%          88.000000
75%          99.000000
max         180.000000
Name: value, dtype: float64

6. Build a daily resting heart rate proxy

Because the watch was not worn continuously, this notebook does not fill in missing days.

Instead, it groups each day and averages the lowest 20% of readings to estimate a daily resting heart rate. That reduces the influence of walking, exercise, and random spikes.

In [76]:
hr_aw['date'] = hr_aw['startDate'].dt.date

daily_rest = (
    hr_aw.groupby('date')['value']
    .apply(lambda x: x.nsmallest(max(1, int(len(x)*0.2))).mean())
    .reset_index(name='resting_hr')
)

7. Smooth the daily signal into a monthly trend

The daily proxy can still be noisy, so the notebook summarizes it by month using a median.

This makes it easier to see when the pattern moved from the 100s into the 90s, 80s, or 60s.

In [77]:
daily_rest['date'] = pd.to_datetime(daily_rest['date'])

monthly_rest = (
    daily_rest.groupby(daily_rest['date'].dt.to_period('M'))['resting_hr']
    .median()
    .reset_index()
)

monthly_rest['date'] = monthly_rest['date'].dt.to_timestamp()

monthly_rest.plot(x='date', y='resting_hr')
Out[77]:
<AxesSubplot:xlabel='date'>

8. Prepare yearly comparisons

These columns are helper features for plotting and comparing each calendar year separately.

In [78]:
daily_rest['year'] = daily_rest['date'].dt.year
daily_rest['day_of_year'] = daily_rest['date'].dt.dayofyear

9. Plot one chart per year

Each year gets its own line chart, plus a horizontal dashed line showing that year’s average resting heart rate.

This makes it easier to see whether a given year sat mostly above or below its own average.

In [85]:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

for year, group in daily_rest.groupby('year'):
    plt.figure(figsize=(18, 8))   # ✅ create figure first
    group = group.sort_values('date')
    avg = group['resting_hr'].mean()
    
    plt.plot(group['date'], group['resting_hr'], label='Daily Resting HR')
    plt.axhline(avg, linestyle='--', label=f'Avg: {avg:.1f}')
    
    plt.grid(True)
    plt.xticks(rotation=45)
    
    plt.title(f"Resting HR - {year}")
    plt.xlabel("Date")
    plt.ylabel("Resting HR")
    plt.legend()
    
    plt.show()

10. Mark the metformin start period

This cutoff separates dates before August 2025 from dates after August 2025, when metformin began.

Because use was irregular at first, this should be treated as the beginning of a transition period rather than a strict treatment effect.

In [86]:
cutoff = pd.to_datetime("2025-08-01")
In [87]:
before_metformin = daily_rest[daily_rest['date'] < cutoff]
after_metformin  = daily_rest[daily_rest['date'] >= cutoff]

11. Mark the more consistent-use phase

This later cutoff identifies the period after November 2025, when metformin use became more regular.

In [91]:
cutoff_consistent = pd.to_datetime("2025-11-01")

after_consistent = daily_rest[daily_rest['date'] >= cutoff_consistent]

12. Compare before vs. after periods

These plots show the resting heart rate pattern before and after the chosen cutoff, each with its own average reference line.

They are useful for visual comparison, but they do not prove causation because watch wear, daily activity, weight, illness, sleep, and medication adherence can all affect heart rate.

In [92]:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

for label, data in {
    "Before Metformin": before_metformin,
    "After Metformin": after_metformin
}.items():
    
    plt.figure(figsize=(14,6))
    
    data = data.sort_values('date')
    avg = data['resting_hr'].mean()
    
    plt.plot(data['date'], data['resting_hr'], label='Resting HR')
    plt.axhline(avg, linestyle='--', label=f'Avg: {avg:.1f}')
    
    plt.title(label)
    plt.legend()
    plt.grid(True)
    plt.show()
